Details: In one pilot study, biological filtration using biologically activated carbon (BAC) was found to be highly effective in the removal of As(V) (97 percent on average). The high removal of arsenic through the BAC filter was due to the presence of iron oxidizing bacteria (e.g. Gallionella) in the BAC filter. Iron present in the raw water was oxidized by the bacteria, and arsenic was removed by co-precipitation with biologically-oxidized iron [236]. At full-scale, however, the removal of total arsenic and As(III) using biological filtration was found to be less effective, with removals of 0 and 22 percent, respectively [2714, 2715]. A bench-scale study observed moderate removal of total arsenic (up to 69 percent) using a pyrolusite and quartzite media. The same study saw up to 77 percent removal of As(III) with the same media [2715].
